He looked at Safiya’s perforated fingers. "I have a scab on my knee," Baz offered. "It’s nearly ready to come off." The Collector staring at him. "That is revolting. Even by my standards." "Fresh flow only," The Collector insisted. Baz sighed. He picked up the sewing needle Safiya had used. "For the lore," he whispered. He jabbed his thumb. "Ow! That actually hurts! In games, you just lose HP bars. This stings!" He squeezed a meager amount of blood into the vial. The Collector capped all three vials and slotted them into loops on his belt. He tapped the POS terminal. It printed a long, curling receipt. He ripped it off and handed it to Leo, who was currently holding a wad of tissues to his face, looking like a war victim. "Here you go," The Collector said. "Transaction Reference #666-PAID. Keep that for your records. If you lose it, I can't reissue. And try not to die for at least twenty-four hours. The paperwork gets complicated if the client expires immediately after payment." He turned to Arthur Pendelton. The old man was shrinking back into his recliner, looking very small and very old. The bravado was gone. He watched The Collector with wide, watery eyes. "Now then, Arthur," The Collector said, his voice dropping an octave. It wasn't the bored bureaucrat voice anymore. It was deeper. Older. "Thirty litres. That’s the contract. Plus the interest. Plus the deception penalty." "I don't have thirty litres," Arthur whispered. "I'll die." "Yes," The Collector agreed pleasantly. "That is generally how exsanguination works." "But the caravan," Arthur mumbled. "I have a caravan." "You can’t tow a caravan to the Underworld, Arthur. The parking is terrible." The Collector stepped toward him. "This won't take long. But it won't be comfortable either." He looked back at the trio of teenagers. "You lot. Clear off. This part isn't for civilians. Health and Safety. Distress causes splash-back." "We’re leaving," Safiya said instantly. She grabbed Leo’s arm. "We are leaving right now." "Bye Arthur!" Baz waved awkwardly. "Thanks for... the trauma!" Leo looked at the old man. Arthur didn't look evil anymore. He just looked terrified. "Sorry," Leo mumbled through his mashed nose. Arthur didn't reply. He was staring at the grey hands reaching for him. The teenagers scrambled out of the room. As the door clicked shut behind them, the volume of the TV suddenly cranked up. *The Chase* theme tune blared through the wood, masking any other sounds that might have come from inside. Safiya didn't stop marching until they were in the lobby. "Out," she commanded. "Outside. Fresh air." They burst out of the automatic doors into the rain. It was still pouring. It was cold, dark, and miserable. It was the most beautiful weather Leo had ever seen. He tilted his head back, letting the rain wash the blood off his chin. It stung his nose, but he didn't care. "We’re alive," Leo said. His voice sounded nasally and weird. "We actually survived." "I need antiseptic," Safiya was examining her fingers under the streetlamp. "And a tetanus shot. And legal counsel." She looked at Leo. "Your nose is crooked." "It gives me character," Leo winced, touching it gingerly. "You possess a very strong right hook, Safiya." "I panicked," she admitted. Then she paused. "No, I didn't. I calculated the fastest route to blood extraction. It was a strategic strike." "You punched me." "You're welcome." "Guys," Baz said, looking at his phone. "My battery is on 1%. But guess what?" "What?" "Arthur’s Wikipedia page just updated. Look." He held up the screen. It was a stub page for *Arthur Pendelton (Gambler)*. Under 'Status', it had changed from 'Alive' to 'Deceased'. "That was fast," Leo shivered. "The admin is efficient." "We should go," Safiya said. "Before he finishes the paperwork and decides to charge us an exit fee." They began to walk toward the bus stop. They were soaked, injured, and traumatized. They walked in silence for a minute. Then Leo’s pocket buzzed. Everyone froze. Safiya stopped. "Leo. Don't." "It buzzed," Leo said, his eyes wide. "It’s the phone. I didn't block the number yet." "Throw it away!" Baz yelled. "Throw it in the drain!" "I can't! It’s on a contract! My mum pays the bill!" Leo reached into his pocket with a shaking hand. He pulled out the phone. The screen was cracked, smeared with rain and a smear of his own blood. One new message. **From: Unknown Number** **Subject: Feedback Request** Leo read it out loud. "How did we do? Please rate your collection experience on a scale of 1 to 5 skulls." He stared at the screen. "Rate it," Baz said. "Give him one star. The customer service was terrible. He was rude." "If you give him one star," Safiya warned, "he might come back to appeal the rating. Managers care about their KPIs." Leo hesitated. His thumb hovered over the screen. He pressed **5**. **Thank you for your feedback. We value your blood.** "I gave him five stars," Leo said, shoving the phone back in his pocket. "I just want him to be happy. I want him to stay away." "Coward," Baz muttered. "Survivor," Leo corrected. The bus arrived. It was the N89 again. The same driver was behind the wheel. He looked at them—a girl with bleeding fingers, a boy with a broken nose, and a boy looking paranoid. "You lot again," the driver sighed. "Don't bleed on the seats." "We won't," Leo promised, climbing aboard. "We've given enough at the office."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a confident, well-executed piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me break down its strengths and weaknesses systematically. **Voice and Tone** The chapter maintains a consistent, dry, deadpan voice throughout. The comedy lands reliably because it emerges from character logic rather than authorial intrusion. Lines like "You can't tow a caravan to the Underworld, Arthur. The parking is terrible" feel genuinely earned — they're funny because The Collector's bureaucratic weariness is so thoroughly established. The bus driver's final line ("Don't bleed on the seats") and Leo's response ("We've given enough at the office") are genuinely good comic writing, the kind of joke that rewards the reader for having followed the whole story. **Character Consistency** The characters are remarkably faithful to their profiles. Safiya's "strategic strike" self-correction ("No, I didn't. I calculated the fastest route to blood extraction") is a perfect encapsulation of her rigidity and refusal to admit vulnerability. Baz's "For the lore" whisper before jabbing himself is exactly right — delusional, self-dramatizing, and oddly endearing. Leo's five-star review out of pure conflict-avoidance is the ideal resolution for his character: not heroism, not growth, just survival through appeasement. The Collector's shift from bored bureaucrat to something genuinely older and darker when addressing Arthur is a well-handled tonal shift that adds texture without undermining the comedy. **Plot Execution vs. Plan** The chapter largely follows the plan, though it diverges in one significant way: the plan specified that Safiya punches Leo to extract the blood for the admin fee, and that this is the climax of Chapter 8 (a separate chapter). Here, the model has collapsed what was planned as Chapter 8 into Chapter 7, combining the nose-punch, the collection, Arthur's fate, and the feedback text into one chapter. This is actually a reasonable editorial decision — the story flows better without an artificial break — but it does mean the chapter is doing double duty and the Arthur resolution feels slightly rushed. His fate is handled efficiently but without the weight it might deserve; the TV volume cranking up is a clever touch, but we move past it quickly. **Dialogue** The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. It's specific, character-driven, and avoids the generic. "I have a scab on my knee. It's nearly ready to come off" is a genuinely funny, character-specific line. The exchange about the five-star review ("Coward" / "Survivor") is punchy and thematically resonant without being heavy-handed. There are no speeches, no exposition dumps, no characters explaining things they wouldn't naturally explain. **Show vs. Tell** The chapter shows rather than tells effectively. Leo's emotional state is conveyed through physical detail (tilting his head back in the rain, the blood washing off his chin) rather than stated. The Wikipedia page updating is a clever, economical way to confirm Arthur's fate without dwelling on it. **Weaknesses** The opening metaphor — "the sickening wet snap of cartonage colliding with biology" — is slightly overwrought. "Cartonage" is an unusual word choice (typically referring to Egyptian mummy casings) that feels like it's reaching for something more poetic than the moment requires. It's a minor stumble. The Baz scab joke is good, but the follow-up ("In games, you just lose HP bars") is slightly on-the-nose given his established character — it's the kind of line that tells us what we already know about him rather than revealing something new. The Arthur resolution, while handled with appropriate darkness, moves a little quickly. The moment where Leo says "Sorry" and Arthur doesn't reply is genuinely affecting, but it's over in two lines. Given how much work went into establishing Arthur as a nasty piece of work, a slightly longer beat here might have deepened the moral ambiguity the plan was aiming for. **Overall** This is skilled, confident comic writing that executes its concept with discipline. The humor is earned, the characters are consistent, and the ending (five-star review, bus driver callback) is genuinely satisfying. The chapter's main weakness is compression — it's doing the work of two planned chapters and occasionally shows the strain — but the quality of execution is high throughout. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The waiting room of St. Jude’s A&E department was a liminal space that existed outside of normal time. It was 2:00 AM. The fluorescent lights hummed with a headache-inducing frequency. The plastic chairs were bolted to the floor in rows of rigid discomfort. Leo sat in row C, holding a chemical ice pack to his face. His nose had stopped bleeding, but it had swollen to the size and colour of a majestic plum. Safiya sat next to him, her fingers bandaged in neat, professional white strips. She was reading a battered copy of *Car & Driver* magazine from 2014 because she had finished all her own reading material. Baz was asleep on the floor, using his rucksack as a pillow. He was snoring. "How long has it been?" Leo asked. His voice sounded like he was speaking through a tuba. "Four hours," Safiya turned a page. "Triage nurse said non-life-threatening facial trauma is Priority 4. We are behind the guy who superglued his hand to a mug and the woman who thinks she swallowed a wasp." Leo sighed, which hurt his face. "At least it’s warm." "It’s tepid," Safiya corrected. "But it is demon-free." The automatic doors slid open. A cold draft swept in. Leo flinched, his eyes darting to the entrance. It was just a paramedic pushing a wheelchair. "Relax," Safiya said without looking up. "The ticket is closed. You got the receipt." Leo patted his pocket. The receipt from the POS terminal was still there, crinkled and damp. He treated it like a passport. "I keep thinking about Arthur," Leo said quietly. "Do you think... do you think it hurt?" Safiya closed the magazine. She looked at the scuffed lino floor. "The Collector said thirty litres. The human body holds five. So... yes. I imagine the physics of that are uncomfortable." "He was a bad person," Leo tried to convince himself. "He tried to kill me. He tried to make me pay his debt." "He was a desperate person," Safiya said. "Desperate people do stupid things. Like punching their classmates." She glanced at his nose. "I really am sorry, Leo. It wasn't personal. It was calculus." "It’s okay," Leo shifted the ice pack. "I’d rather have a broken nose than be a husk in a nursing home." "Husk," Baz muttered in his sleep. "Dry husk. No loot drops." Leo looked at his phone. It was sitting on his lap. He hadn't unlocked it since the bus. "I need to change my number," Leo said. "I’m going to O2 tomorrow. I’m going to get a new SIM. I don't care if I lose my data." "Good idea," Safiya nodded. "Get a contract this time. Pay-as-you-go is clearly unregulated." Leo picked up the phone. He intended to turn it off. To power it down and never look at it again until he could throw it into the canal. But as he lifted it, the screen lit up. Not a text. An email notification. **From: St. Jude’s School - Administration** **Subject: Unauthorised Absence / Library Incident** Leo groaned. "The school knows." Safiya sat up straighter. "Let me see." She leaned over. "Oh no. Mrs. Gable filed a report about the carpet stain. It says 'Student Leo P. caused biohazard incident in non-fiction section'." "And look," Leo scrolled down. "Geography Department. 'Failure to attend Mock Exam B results in automatic U grade'." "A 'U'?" Safiya looked more horrified by this than she had by the demon. "That drags down the school average! Leo, you have to appeal. We can claim extenuating circumstances." "What circumstances?" Leo laughed, a wheezing sound. "Sorry, Miss, I couldn't locate the coastal erosion because I was being hunted by a debt collector from Hell?" "Medical emergency," Safiya said firmly. "Acute nasal trauma. We have the hospital wristbands to prove it. I’ll draft the email. I’ll use formally aggressive language." Leo looked at her. She was exhausted. Her blazer was rumpled, her hair was frizzing in the damp air, and she had bandages on her fingers because she’d stabbed herself with a needle to save him. "Thanks, Safiya," he said. She looked at him, surprised. Then she shrugged, smoothing her skirt. "I can't have you failing. It reflects poorly on my leadership." "Right. Obviously." "Leo?" A nurse called out from the desk. "Leo Perkins?" "That’s me," Leo stood up. His legs were stiff. "Cubicle 4, please." Leo gathered his stuff. He kicked Baz’s shoe. "Wake up. We’re moving." Baz snorted and sat up, wiping drool from his chin. "Did we win? Is the boss dead?" "Go back to sleep, Baz." Leo walked toward the double doors leading to the treatment area. He paused at the threshold and looked back at the waiting room. The vending machine in the corner was flickering. For a second, just a split second, the hum of the refrigerator sounded like a gravelly sigh. Leo froze. He stared at the vending machine. Inside, a packet of crisps fell from shelf D4 without anyone touching it. *Clunk.* Leo waited. He held his breath. Was it him? Was The Collector back for a snack penalty? Nothing happened. The machine just hummed. "Leo?" Safiya called. "Coming," Leo said. He walked through the doors. *** Two days later. Leo stood on the bridge over the canal. It was, inevitably, raining. He held the phone in his hand. He had already transferred his photos to the cloud. He had backed up his contacts (deleting Arthur’s number, obviously). "Do it," Baz said. He was leaning on the railing, throwing pebbles into the murky water. "Cast it into the fire. Or the sludge. Whatever." "It’s a perfectly good phone," Leo hesitated. "Ideally, I should recycle it. E-waste is a massive problem." "Leo," Safiya said. She was standing holding an umbrella over him, because she refused to get wet. "That device has processed a demonic transaction. It is tainted. Throw it." "Right." Leo took a breath. He looked at the screen one last time. No new messages. Just a low battery warning. He pulled his arm back and hurled it. The phone spun through the air, end over end. It hit the water with a satisfying *plop* and vanished beneath the brown surface. "Gone," Leo dusted his hands. "End of chapter." "Until someone goes magnet fishing and dredges it up," Baz mused. "And then the curse passes to them. Like *The Ring*." "Shut up, Baz," Leo said. They turned to walk away. "So," Leo adjusted the plastic splint on his nose. "Maths mock tomorrow." "I've made you a revision schedule," Safiya said, pulling a folded piece of paper from her pocket. "You’ve missed two days of prep. You need to do three hours tonight to catch up." "Three hours? Safiya, I’m in convalescence." "You can do algebra while lying down. I checked." They walked along the towpath. For the first time in three days, Leo didn't check behind him. He didn't look for grey vans. He didn't flinch at the sound of a text message. He was just a teenager with a broken nose, bad grades, and terrifying friends. As they rounded the corner, passing a row of damp terraced houses, a postman was walking up a garden path. He was wearing a bright red Royal Mail jacket. He was whistling. Leo stopped. He watched the postman push a letter through a door. The postman turned. He saw Leo watching him. He wasn't grey. He was pink-cheeked and human. He smiled and nodded. "Afternoon." "Afternoon," Leo replied, letting out a breath he didn't know he was holding. The postman walked past them. As he passed, Leo noticed something. Tucked into the postman’s belt, next to his scanner, was a small, clear plastic vial. It was empty. Leo blinked. He turned around. "Did you see that?" "See what?" Baz asked. "The vial. On his belt." "Probably for hand sanitizer," Safiya said, not looking back. "Come on. If we miss the 4:15 bus, we have to wait twenty minutes." Leo looked back. The postman turned the corner and vanished. "Yeah," Leo said, facing forward. "Hand sanitizer. Probably." He started walking. He kept his hands in his pockets. He kept his head down. And for the rest of his life, Leo Perkins always, *always* paid his bills on time. **END**
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the concluding chapter of "The Red Deletion," and it largely delivers on the story's core promise: a darkly comic, bureaucratically mundane resolution to a supernatural crisis. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter's greatest achievement is its tonal consistency. The A&E waiting room is rendered with genuine specificity — the bolted plastic chairs, the 2014 Car & Driver magazine, the man who superglued his hand to a mug — these details feel authentically British and authentically mundane. The chapter earns its comedy through accumulation of small, recognizable humiliations rather than through jokes. The character voices are largely consistent with their profiles. Safiya's response to the school email ("A 'U'? That drags down the school average!") is perfectly calibrated — she's more horrified by the academic consequence than by the demon. Her line "I can't have you failing. It reflects poorly on my leadership" when Leo thanks her is exactly right: it refuses sentimentality while still conveying warmth through the cracks. Baz's sleep-talking ("Dry husk. No loot drops") is a small gem. Leo's passivity and catastrophic thinking remain consistent. The chapter plan called for Safiya to punch Leo in the nose as the "admin fee" collection method, and the aftermath of this is handled well — the ice pack, the plum-sized nose, the matter-of-fact apology ("It wasn't personal. It was calculus"). This is good darkly comic writing. The ending — the postman with the vial — is appropriately ambiguous and unsettling without being overwrought. The final line ("Leo Perkins always, always paid his bills on time") is a clean, earned button that delivers the story's non-moral moral without moralizing. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan specified that the ending text would be a customer satisfaction survey ("Rate your collection experience"), and Leo would throw the phone into a medical waste bin. The actual chapter changes this: the survey text is absent, and instead Leo throws the phone into a canal two days later. This is a significant deviation from the plan. The survey gag was genuinely funny and on-brand; its absence is a loss. The canal scene is competent but less sharp. The Arthur section is handled awkwardly. The chapter implies Arthur died ("thirty litres... the physics of that are uncomfortable"), but this is handled very obliquely. The plan didn't specify Arthur's death, and the chapter seems to be improvising here. Safiya's line about the physics is clever, but the moral weight of Arthur's apparent death is neither fully confronted nor fully dismissed — it sits in an uncomfortable middle ground that doesn't quite serve the story's stated "no moral lesson" ethos. The vending machine moment — the crisps falling, Leo's paranoia — is effective as a beat, but it's slightly over-explained. The narration tells us Leo is wondering if it's "a snack penalty," which is funny, but the moment would land harder with slightly less hand-holding. The dialogue is generally strong but occasionally slips into functional exposition. "Triage nurse said non-life-threatening facial trauma is Priority 4" is good. But some exchanges feel like they're moving pieces rather than revealing character. The "liminal space" opening line is a minor purple prose stumble — it's a slightly tired phrase that the rest of the chapter's grounded specificity doesn't need. Baz's "Like The Ring" observation about the phone passing its curse to a magnet fisher is a genuinely good joke that also serves the story's themes. The postman ending adds a nice layer of paranoia, though it risks undermining the "ticket is closed" resolution slightly. It works because it's ambiguous enough to be deniable. OVERALL: This is a competent, often genuinely funny conclusion that maintains the story's voice and delivers most of what the plan promised. It's not a masterpiece — the deviation from the plan's best gag (the survey text) and some minor over-explanation hold it back — but it's a solid piece of darkly comic YA writing with real personality. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a confident, energetic piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. The author has a strong command of comic timing, a distinctive voice, and a clear understanding of the genre conventions they're working within. Let me break down the strengths and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND TONE: The piece establishes its voice immediately and maintains it with impressive consistency across eight chapters. The narrator's dry, self-aware British humour lands reliably. Lines like "Coasts were safe. Coasts didn't text you. Coasts just eroded" and "He felt like he was eroding too" demonstrate genuine wit. The comedy of bureaucratic horror — a demon worried about parking tickets, complaining about traffic, reading the Metro horoscope — is well-executed and consistently funny. This is the piece's greatest strength. CHARACTER: Leo is a believable, well-drawn protagonist. His instinct to play dead, his catastrophising, his cowardice punctuated by moments of genuine panic-driven action — these feel authentic to a seventeen-year-old. Baz functions well as comic relief, though he's somewhat one-dimensional (the enthusiastic lore-keeper who quotes games). Safiya is the most interesting character — competent, terrifying, and ultimately revealed to have genuine warmth beneath the bureaucratic exterior. The Collector is an excellent creation: a bored, put-upon civil servant of the underworld who complains about double yellow lines and tight pipe squeezes. Arthur Pendelton is used effectively as a dark mirror — a desperate person who made a bad deal, not a cartoon villain. PLOT AND PACING: The plot is propulsive and well-structured. Each chapter escalates the situation logically while maintaining the comic tone. The resolution — a digital handshake via forced thumb-press — is clever and consistent with the established rules. The cancellation fee twist is a nice subversion of the "we won" moment. The ending, with the postman and the vial, is appropriately ambiguous and earns its darkness. However, there are some pacing issues. Chapter 5 (the nursing home corridor) drags slightly before reaching Arthur. The tech support sequence in Chapter 5 is funny but goes on a beat too long. Chapter 8 (the A&E waiting room) is the weakest chapter — it functions as a denouement but feels padded, and the vending machine moment is a bit too on-the-nose. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is generally strong and character-consistent. The Collector's dialogue is particularly good — weary, professional, and menacing. The exchanges between Leo and Safiya crackle. Baz's lines are reliably funny if occasionally repetitive. Arthur's dialogue ("I have a psychic shotgun") is excellent. The weakness is that Baz occasionally exists purely to deliver exposition or lore-commentary, which makes him feel like a narrative device rather than a person. METAPHORS AND PROSE: The metaphors are mostly earned and land well. "His heart hammering against his ribs like a trapped bird" is a cliché, but most of the imagery is fresher. "The P4 bus stop was an ecosystem of misery" works. The description of the Clay Man as looking like "a child's drawing of a van, rendered in matte grey plastic" is genuinely evocative. There are occasional purple moments — "the kinetic energy of a thermonuclear weapon about to detonate" for Safiya's anger is slightly overwrought — but these are infrequent. CONSISTENCY AND COHERENCE: The internal logic of the world is well-maintained. The rules of the debt transfer are established and followed. The Collector's limitations (can't enter school, bound by transit bylaws, needs a warrant) are consistent. The digital handshake resolution follows from established rules. This is impressive for a long piece. WEAKNESSES: The piece occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states. "Leo felt sick. Not just bus-sick, but soul-sick" is a minor example. The Arthur resolution, while darkly comic, sidesteps the moral weight of essentially condemning an old man to death — the piece gestures at this ("He just looked terrified") but doesn't fully commit to the darkness, which slightly undercuts the "darkly comic" brief. The final chapter's A&E section is the weakest, feeling like an epilogue that goes on too long before the canal scene provides a better ending. OVERALL: This is a genuinely accomplished piece of comic fiction. It's funny, consistent, well-paced for the most part, and demonstrates real craft. It's not a masterpiece — the characters could be deeper, the darkness could be darker — but it's well above average and would sit comfortably in the YA market it's targeting. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 13 Pacing: 14 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 78.2/100
+ Space Western — Cargo Unknown (Chapter Avg: 66.9 | Final: 64.7)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something&#x27;s... moving in that crate we picked up. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
Here is a comprehensive writing plan for a Space Western short story titled **"The Rattle in the Hold."** # Brainstorming **Tone & Atmosphere:** * *Visuals:* Gritty, industrial sci-fi. Rusted bulkheads, flickering halogen lights, dust-covered spacesuits, revolvers modified with plasma coils. A melding of *Firefly* warmth and *Alien* claustrophobia. * *Soundscape:* The hum of a struggling engine, the twang of an acoustic guitar, the unnerving silence of space, the specific "thud" coming from the crate. * *Themes:* Greed vs. Morality, the obsolete vs. the new, "family" found in outcasts, the consequences of frontier justice. **Character Concepts:** * **Captain Silas "Rusty" Vance:** A former soldier on the losing side of a galactic civil war. Chaotic good. Buried in debt to a crime syndicate. He drives the plot through his need for money. * **The Pilot - "Jax":** A twitchy, superstitious navigator who talks to the ship. Provides comic relief and tension. * **The Muscle - Mara:** Large, silent, carrying a heavy kinetic hammer. Deeply loyal but pragmatic. * **The Antagonist - Marshal Kray:** A corrupt lawman working for a mining corporation, trailing the crew. **The "Crate" (The MacGuffin) - Potential Options:** 1. *The Beast:* An illegal, venomous alien predator for a gladiator pit. (High action/Horror). 2. *The VIP:* A runaway corporate heir or a "living weapon" child in stasis. (Emotional/Moral dilemma). 3. *The Legend:* The crate contains a sentient, ancient android that knows the location of a "Lost Dutchman's Mine" (a planet made of platinum). (Adventure/Mystery). **Plot Arcs - Possible Directions:** * *Concept A (Horror Western):* They open the crate, the creature escapes, and it becomes a hunt through the dark corridors of the ship while they are being pursued by the law. * *Concept B (The Escort Mission):* The "movement" is a person waking up. They realize they’ve been hired to traffic a person. The Captain decides to break the contract, putting a bounty on their heads. * *Concept C (The Gold Fever):* The crate contains a creature that psychically amplifies greed. The crew starts turning on each other while the "law" closes in. **Pacing for 8 Chapters:** * Needs to start *in media res* or immediately after the job. * The "Reveal" of the crate's contents must happen by Chapter 2 or 3. * Midpoint twist needed around Chapter 4/5. * Climax must involve a standoff (classic Western shootout) or a space maneuver. # Reflection **Critiquing the Ideas:** * *Idea 1 (The Beast/Horror):* While fun, a pure "monster in the house" story might struggle to fill 8 chapters (8,000 words) with meaningful dialogue and character growth. It risks becoming repetitive (hide, run, shoot). * *Idea 3 (The Legend/Android):* This feels the most "Space Western." The trope of the "Lost Mine" is classic Western, but making the map a *living thing* adds the Sci-Fi twist. It allows for character interaction with the object, rather than just running from it. * *Idea 2 (The VIP):* This is very similar to *The Mandalorian* or *Firefly*. To avoid being a clone, we need to make the "passenger" dangerous or morally ambiguous. **Narrowing the Focus:** Let's go with a variation of **Idea 3** combined with **Idea 1**. The crate contains a **"Diviner"**—a bio-engineered organism that looks like a nondescript animal (maybe a robotic armadillo or a synthetic bird) that can "sniff out" refined coaxium (fuel/gold). * *The Conflict:* It’s not just a map; it’s a magnet for trouble. The creature is erratic and eats the ship's fuel if not contained. * *The Twist:* The creature isn't just a fuel-sniffer; it's property of a warlord, and it has a tracking beacon embedded in its gut. * *Why this works:* It keeps the crew together on the ship (bottle episode feel), creates internal tension (the creature eats their resources), and external tension (the warlord is coming). **Refining the "Movement":** The prompt is "something's moving." If it's a person, the movement is deliberate. If it's an animal, it's scuttling. Let's make it **scuttling.** It implies something feral and unpredictable. **Final Decision for Story Beat:** * **The Setup:** A desperate crew takes a "no questions asked" cargo job. * **The Inciting Incident:** The crate rumbles. They open it. * **The Contents:** A "Void-Basilisk"—rare, illegal, dangerous, but valuable. * **The Complication:** It escapes into the ship's ventilation *and* the client refuses to pay because they "opened the package." Now they have a monster loose and no payday, with the client coming to kill them to silence witnesses. ***Wait, let's pivot slightly for better emotional resonance.*** Let's make the contents a **prototype law-enforcement droid** that has "gone rogue" and has a Western personality subroutine. It thinks it's a Sheriff. This allows for dialogue. The crew are outlaws. The "package" wakes up and tries to arrest them on their own ship. This allows for humor, action, and a unique dynamic. **Selection:** **"The Iron Lawman."** The crate contains an archaic, heavy-duty peacekeeping droid (Unit 0-N3 "One") scheduled for smelting. It wakes up. It is dangerous, rigid, but ultimately develops a code of honor. # 8-Chapter Writing Plan (Outline) ### Chapter 1: The Blind Haul * **Goal:** Establish the setting, the desperation of the crew, and the prompt. * **Plot:** Captain Vance and his crew (Pilot Jinx, Mechanic Hobb) depart a shady spaceport with a heavy, sealed magnetic crate. They are low on fuel and food. Tension is high. * **Ending Hook:** While running silent through an asteroid belt to avoid scans, Hobb calls the bridge: "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up." ### Chapter 2: Pandora’s Box * **Goal:** The Reveal. * **Plot:** Arguments on the bridge. Vance goes down to the hold. The movement is rhythmic, metallic clanging. They debate engaging the airlock vs. opening it. Greed wins (they want to know if the goods are damaged). They crack the seal. * **Ending Hook:** The crate opens. A rusted, skeletal hand grabs the edge. A monolithic droid sits up, wearing a tattered poncho. It scans Vance. "You are in violation of Sector Code 9. Drop your weapon." ### Chapter 3: Standoff in the Mess Hall * **Goal:** Establish the dynamic/Humor/Threat. * **Plot:** The Droid (Rust-Bucket) is damaged and low on power, so it can't physically overpower them yet, but it's incredibly durable. They manage to corral it into the mess hall. They realize this is a "Sheriff-Bot" from 100 years ago. Extremely rare antique, worth a fortune, but it keeps trying to read them their rights. * **Ending Hook:** A perimeter alarm sounds. The people who hired them to transport the crate (The Syndicate) aren't waiting for delivery—they are intercepting. ### Chapter 4: The Double Cross * **Goal:** External Conflict. * **Plot:** The Syndicate ship hails them. It turns out the crate wasn't being transported; it was being *disposed of* or hidden, and the Syndicate decided it's cheaper to blow up Vance’s ship than pay the fee. Space battle ensues. The *Copperhead* takes heavy damage. * **Ending Hook:** To survive, they need someone to man the manual gun turrets. Hobb is injured. The only other "person" capable of aiming is the Sheriff-Bot. ### Chapter 5: Deputized * **Goal:** The Team-Up. * **Plot:** Vance cuts a deal with the Bot. "Help us survive, and I submit to your jurisdiction later." The Bot processes this logic and agrees, "deputizing" Vance temporarily. High-octane action sequence where the Bot uses its aim-bot protocols to save the ship. * **Ending Hook:** They escape into a nebula, but the ship is dead in the water. The Bot’s power is critical. ### Chapter 6: Fireside Chat * **Goal:** Character depth/Pacing breath. * **Plot:** Drifting in the dark. The crew and the Bot repair the ship. Philosophical dialogue between the outlaw Captain and the Lawman Bot about justice vs. survival. The Bot reveals why it was crated: It refused a corrupt order from a human superior. It has "free will," which is why it’s dangerous. * **Ending Hook:** The Syndicate tracks their ion trail. They aren't just sending a ship; they are sending a Boarding Party. ### Chapter 7: The Alamo * **Goal:** The Climax. * **Plot:** The Syndicate breaches the airlock. A close-quarters shoot-out in the corridors of the ship. Hobb and Jinx are cornered. The Sheriff-Bot takes massive damage shielding the crew, acting against its self-preservation protocols (breaking its programming). Vance fights like a demon. * **Ending Hook:** The Syndicate leader is taken down, but the Bot is critically damaged, leaking fluid and sparking. ### Chapter 8: Sunset Protocol * **Goal:** Resolution and Theme. * **Plot:** The dust settles. The crew is safe. The Bot is dying (memory core corruption). Vance has a chance to wipe the Bot's memory and sell the chassis for the fortune they need, or repair the "person" and remain in debt. * **Resolution:** Vance uses their last resources to patch the Bot, keeping its personality intact. They drop the "Sheriff" off at a neutral moon where it can be the Law for a mining town that needs one. * **Final Image:** The crew flies off, still broke, eating algae paste, but with a clean conscience. Space Western closure.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Core Concept & Plot** The story will center on the "Iron Lawman" concept: a desperate crew discovers a deactivated, archaic peace-keeping android in their cargo. The narrative arc follows the crew transforming from scavengers looking for a payout into hesitant heroes, catalyzed by the robot's rigid but noble moral code. The plot is a "bottle episode" intended to create claustrophobia, forcing the crew and the dangerous cargo to coexist until the external threat (the client) forces a truce. **Voice & Tone** * **Voice:** Third-person limited, anchored in Captain Silas "Rusty" Vance. The narration will mimic the grit of the setting—laconic, slightly cynical, but observational. * **Style:** "Used Future" aesthetic. I intend to focus heavily on sensory details: the smell of ozone, the groan of metal, the flickering of failing hydraulics. Dialogue will mix Sci-Fi terminology with Western slang (e.g., "slagged," "mag-spurs," "reckon"). * **Pacing:** The story will start with suspense (horror vibes), transition into a comedy of errors (the standoff), escalate into high-action (space combat), and end on a quiet, character-driven note. **Aims & Avoidance** * **Aim:** To explore the definition of "outlaw"—is it a legal status or a moral one? * **Aim:** To create a distinct personality for the android that isn't just a Data or C-3PO clone; he should feel like an old, retired gunfighter trapped in metal. * **Avoid:** Melodramatic backstory dumps. The war background will be implied, not explained. * **Avoid:** Making the crew too incompetent. They are professionals, just unlucky. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: The Blind Haul** * **Setting:** The cockpit and cargo hold of *The Copperhead*. * **Action:** Captain Vance and his pilot, Jax, are navigating a treacherous asteroid field (The Boneyard) to stay off the grid. They are exhausted and broke. The atmosphere is tense; the ship is barely holding together. * **Key Beat:** Discussion of the cargo—a "Level 5 Containment" crate picked up from a shady mining depot. No questions asked, high payout. * **Ending:** Mechanic Mara buzzes the comms from the hold. The prompt is delivered: "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up." **Chapter 2: The Wake-Up Call** * **Setting:** The cargo hold. * **Action:** Vance descends to investigate. The crate is vibrating. Fearing a biological infestation (space rats or worse), they prep weapons. Driven by the need to ensure the goods aren't damaging themselves (and forfeiting the pay), they crack the seal. * **Key Beat:** The reveal. It’s not a beast, but a massive, cowboy-esque android (Unit 0-N3, or "Owen"). It activates, disarms Vance with frightening speed, and declares the crew under arrest for transport of stolen property. * **Ending:** A standoff. The android is low on power and stalls, but blocks the exit. **Chapter 3: Jurisdiction** * **Setting:** The Mess Hall / Ship Corridors. * **Action:** A game of cat and mouse. The crew tries to disable the android without destroying it (realizing it's an antique "Marshal" unit worth a fortune). The Android, operating on corrupted logic, tries to "process" them while struggling with his own failing battery. * **Key Beat:** Comedy/Character moment. The Android tries to read them their rights but glitches, reciting mining regulations instead. They realize he isn't malfunctioning; he was *discarded* for having a conscience. * **Ending:** The ship’s proximity alarms blare. A heavy cruiser, *The Syndicate*, drops out of warp. **Chapter 4: Burn Notice** * **Setting:** The Cockpit. * **Action:** The client hails them. It’s a trap. They didn't want the crate transported; they wanted it taken to deep space and the ship scuttled to erase the evidence. The Android reveals he holds evidence of a massacre the Syndicate committed. * **Key Beat:** Vance tries to negotiate, but the Syndicate opens fire. The *Copperhead* takes a massive hit. Gravity fails. * **Ending:** Vance realizes they can't man the guns and fly the ship simultaneously. He needs the Android. **Chapter 5: Deputized** * **Setting:** The ship under siege/Zero-G. * **Action:** Vance offers the Android a deal: "Help us fight, and I submit to judgment later." The Android processes the logic: The Syndicate are murderers + Self Defense = Authorized use of lethal force. * **Key Beat:** The Android plugs directly into the ship's gunnery mainframe. His aim is perfect. The tide of the space battle turns. * **Ending:** The Syndicate ship is disabled, but they launch boarding pods before going dark. They are coming in on foot. **Chapter 6: The Code** * **Setting:** Preparation for boarding. * **Action:** A lull in the action. The crew and the Android barricade the airlock. * **Key Beat:** A quiet conversation between Vance and "Owen" (the android). Owen explains why he was crated: he refused an order to fire on striking workers. He asks Vance if he is a "bad man." Vance admits he's done bad things, but he has a line he won't cross. * **Ending:** The airlock blows. The breach begins. **Chapter 7: Iron and Lead** * **Setting:** The corridors of *The Copperhead*. * **Action:** Intense close-quarters combat. Laser fire, kinetics, and grit. Mara uses her hammer; Jax is pinned down. * **Key Beat:** Owen steps into the line of fire to protect Jax, taking a plasma round to his chest chassis. He fights brutally, not as a machine, but as a protector. * **Ending:** The boarders are repelled. The threat is neutralized. Owen collapses, his core systems failing. **Chapter 8: The Sunset Protocol** * **Setting:** A repair dock on a neutral moon. * **Action:** The aftermath. The crew is safe, but impoverished again. They stripped the Syndicate pods for supplies. * **Key Beat:** The decision. They could sell Owen for scrap and retire. Instead, Vance spends their last credits on a new power core for him. * **Resolution:** They reactivate Owen. He asks if he should arrest them now. Vance points out they are outside his jurisdiction. Owen agrees, "logging" the incident as time served. He stays on the moon to protect the locals. * **Final Image:** The *Copperhead* blast off into the stars, poor but free. Vance touches his hat to the screen as the Iron Lawman watches them leave.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
This is a crucial step. Space Westerns, by their nature, rely on texture, grit, and gray morality. LLMs, by their nature, lean toward smoothness, clarity, and black-and-white morality. If we execute the plan as it stands without "humanizing" safeguards, we risk creating a generic episode of a Saturday morning cartoon rather than a compelling piece of fiction. Here is a deep dive into the Human vs. LLM approach for this specific story. --- # Human vs. LLM Approaches to Fiction ### 1. The "Safety" Bias vs. The Danger of the Frontier * **The LLM Tendency:** LLMs are trained to be helpful and harmless. In fiction, this translates to protagonists who are fundamentally "good" people who unwillingly do bad things only when forced. Violence is usually sanitized or strictly defensive. The characters explain their feelings clearly. * **The Human Approach:** A human writer understands that desperate people (outlaws) are often unlikeable, selfish, or petty. A human writer allows characters to make mistakes that *aren't* redeemed immediately. * **Application to this Story:** An LLM version of Vance fights the Syndicate because "it's the right thing to do." A human version of Vance might fight the Syndicate purely out of spite, or because he realizes he’s dead anyway, or perhaps he even tries to sell the robot out first and fails. ### 2. The "Bow-Tie" Ending vs. The Cost of Living * **The LLM Tendency:** Structure is paramount. The LLM wants to resolve the central tension (the crate), the external tension (the Syndicate), and the internal tension (the crew's poverty) in a way that feels "complete." Chapter 8 currently reads like a TV show reset button—everyone learns a lesson, and the status quo is restored. * **The Human Approach:** Real stories often end with dangling threads or bittersweet victories. Character arcs don't always complete a circle; sometimes they just spiral slightly. * **Application to this Story:** The "Sheriff of a mining town" ending is very neat. A human writer might leave the robot broken in the cargo hold, a permanent burden they can't afford to fix but refuse to scrap. Or, the robot survives but loses his personality, making the victory hollow. ### 3. Dialogue: Exposition vs. Subtext * **The LLM Tendency:** Characters say exactly what they mean. * *LLM Dialogue:* "I am afraid we are going to die, Captain. I regret not seeing my home again." * **The Human Approach:** Characters talk around the point. * *Human Dialogue:* "Hobb, you fix that thermal coil yet?" "Working on it." "Work faster." (Subtext: We are terrified). * **Application to this Story:** The conversation in Chapter 6 (The Code) is the highest risk area. An LLM will write a philosophical lecture about "Free Will." A human approach would likely have them talk about something mundane—like the taste of beans or the quality of the welding on the robot's arm—to imply the connection. --- # Deep Dive Critique of the Current Plan Here is a critique of the **"Iron Lawman"** plan, specifically identifying where it falls into LLM tropes. ### 1. The "Adorkable" Robot Trope (Chapter 2 & 3) * **Propensity:** The plan describes the robot effectively trying to arrest them while glitching. * **The Trap:** It is very easy for an LLM to make this "cute." (e.g., the robot bumps into walls, apologizes, acts like C-3PO). * **The Human Need:** The robot needs to be *scary*. Even if it's glitching, it's a 400-pound war machine. The humor should be dark/dry, not slapstick. The "glitch" shouldn't be a stutter; it should be a terrifying dissonance—like the robot racking a shotgun slide while politely asking for a permit. ### 2. The Predictable Turn (Chapter 5) * **Propensity:** Vance says, "Help us and I'll turn myself in later." The Robot says, "Logic accepted." * **The Trap:** This is a logic puzzle solution. It feels mechanical. * **The Human Need:** The "team up" shouldn't be a transactional deal made with words. It should be desperate. Maybe the robot saves them not because of a logic loop, but because of a corrupted memory file where he thinks Vance is his old partner. This adds tragedy and ambiguity. ### 3. "We Are Not So Different" (Chapter 6) * **Propensity:** The plan calls for a "philosophical dialogue." * **The Trap:** This is the single biggest trap. LLMs love to summarize themes in dialogue. "You are a machine, but you have a soul!" / "You are a man, but you act like a machine!" * **The Human Need:** Cut the philosophy. Have them share a quiet moment of "work." Vance cleaning his gun, the robot welding his own leg. The bond is formed in the silence, or in a story about something unrelated (e.g., the robot remembers a specific sunrise; Vance remembers a woman he disappointed). ### 4. The Villain Problem (Chapter 4 & 7) * **Propensity:** The Syndicate is faceless evil. * **The Trap:** Generic sci-fi bad guys who storm the ship and die. * **The Human Need:** Give the threat a face. Maybe the Syndicate representative on the comms is bored, bureaucratic, just doing a job. It makes the violence more chilling if the enemy isn't cackling, but just filing paperwork to have them executed. --- # Brainstorming Human Elements (To inject into the plan) To pull this away from "AI-generated story" and towards "Human-generated space western," we need to inject specific sensory details, flaws, and subtext. **1. Sensory Grime (The Setting):** * *Smell:* The ship shouldn't just be old; it should smell like recirculated sweat, ozone, and burned coffee. * *Sound:* The crate shouldn't just "rumble." It should make a sound that vibrates the characters' teeth. * *Visual:* The robot isn't shiny. It’s covered in barnacles? Rust? Stickers from previous owners? Make him look like trash. **2. Character Flaws (The Motivation):** * *Vance:* Let him be tempted to sell the robot. In Chapter 3 or 4, have a moment where Vance actually checks the black market price of the robot’s CPU. He considers it. He's not a hero *yet*. * *The Robot (Owen):* Give him a tick. Maybe his audio synthesizer is broken, so he speaks in sampled audio clips from old western movies or public service announcements. This makes him stranger and harder to communicate with, increasing the tension. **3. The Violence (The Action):** * LLM action is usually: "Vance fired his gun. The enemy fell." * Human action is chaotic. Ear-ringing noise. Shots miss and rupture a steam pipe, scalding everyone. The robot doesn't just "shoot"; maybe he physically grabs an enemy and throws them against a bulkhead, denting the metal. Make it visceral and uncomfortable. **4. The Ending (The Revision):** * Instead of the robot becoming a Sheriff (which feels like a spinoff setup), make it messier. * *Idea:* The robot is too damaged to be fixed fully. They manage to save his body, but his "Sheriff" personality is corrupted. He reverts to a factory-default loader bot. They keep him on the ship as heavy labor. It’s a tragedy—Vance knows there was a person in there, but now it's just a machine again. Vance treats the machine with strange respect, which is the only sign of his character growth. **5. "Show, Don't Tell" the Prompt:** * The prompt is "something's moving." * An LLM might say: "They looked fearfully at the crate." * Humanize it: Focus on the cat. The ship has a ship's cat (or vermin). The cat is hissing at the crate. Or the condensation on the crate is being disturbed by heat from the inside. Small details. **Summary of Humanization Strategy for next steps:** * **Dirty up the setting.** * **Complicate the morality (Vance considers betrayal).** * **Remove the philosophical lectures.** * **Make the ending bittersweet/costly, not perfect.**
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
Here is the final writing plan, designed to produce a gritty, character-driven Space Western that avoids typical AI pitfalls. # Intention **Core Narrative & Atmosphere** My intention is to write a story about the "cost of doing business" in a universe that considers life cheap. The tone will be "blue-collar desperation." I plan to avoid the polished, high-tech feel of typical sci-fi in favor of a tactile, rusting environment—leaking pipes, recycled air that smells like ozone and stale coffee, and technology that requires percussive maintenance. **Humanizing the Characters (Breaking Tropes)** * **Captain Vance:** I will write him not as a hero waiting to happen, but as a pragmatic survivor. He will actively consider selling the android or venting the crate to save his own skin. His shift to "heroism" will be born of spite toward the corporation rather than altruism. * **The Android (Unit 0-N3 "Owen"):** I intend to avoid the "Pinocchio" trope (a robot wanting to be human). Owen is a machine running corrupted software. He is dangerous, heavy, and glitchy. His "personality" is a result of decaying code, not a magical soul. He will be terrifying first, and sympathetic second. * **The Antagonist:** I will avoid a mustache-twirling villain. The threat is a bored corporate middle-manager on a viewscreen who simply wants to close a spreadsheet by liquidating "assets" (the crew). **Stylistic Choices** * **Voice:** Third-person limited (Vance). The narration will filter everything through his exhaustion and cynicism. * **Dialogue:** I will avoid exposition dumps. Dialogue will be clipped, functional, and laden with subtext. Characters will talk about repairs and food when they mean "I'm scared." * **Ending:** I will avoid a "bow-tie" resolution. The crew will survive, but they will be poorer and more damaged than when they started. The victory is merely stepping away from the edge of the grave, not finding a pot of gold. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: The Dead Weight** * **Focus:** Atmosphere and Desperation. * **Plot:** Introduce the *Copperhead* limping through the "Boneyard" asteroid belt. Show, don't tell, the financial straits—Vance rationing his last caffeine patch, the thermal couplings failing. Establish the "Package": a crate they were paid double to ask zero questions about. The crew (Vance, Pilot Jax, Mechanic Mara) is on edge, snapping at each other. * **The Prompt:** The chapter ends in the cargo bay. Shadows stretch long. Mara checks the manifest on the comms, voice trembling: *"Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up."* **Chapter 2: The Rust Bucket** * **Focus:** Horror/Tension. * **Plot:** They treat the crate like a bomb. They arm themselves with kinetic hammers and shotguns. It’s not a cute awakening; the crate buckles from the inside. When they crack the seal, a massive, skeletal robotic hand shoots out and throttles Vance. * **Key Beat:** The droid (Unit 0-N3) drags itself out. It’s missing legs (or they are damaged). It’s huge, older than the ship, and terrifying. It scans them, eyes flickering red to blue, and speaks in a distorted audio loop of a chaotic withdrawal order from a forgotten war. It passes out (power failure) before it can kill them. * **Human Element:** Vance’s first reaction isn't curiosity; it's anger that his payout is spoiled. **Chapter 3: The Appraisal** * **Focus:** Greed vs. Survival. * **Plot:** They have the droid mag-locked to the bulkhead. Hobb (the Mechanic) hotwires its battery. They realize what it is: a pre-UniWar Marshal unit. Illegal, dangerous, but the raw palladium in its chassis is worth enough to clear their debt. * **Key Beat:** Vance looks up the black market scrap value. He actively plans to dismantle it. The droid wakes up properly this time. It doesn't act robotic; it has a distinct, weary, "old man" affectation caused by the personality overlay. It accuses them of being grave robbers. * **Conflict:** The ship hails. The Client (Syndicate) is calling. **Chapter 4: Liquidation Asset** * **Focus:** The Banality of Evil. * **Plot:** Vance answers the hail, expecting payment instructions. The Syndicate rep is calm, bored, and eating lunch on screen. He reveals the crate was meant to be dumped into a star, not opened. Since the seal is broken, "containment is breached." He orders the *Copperhead* to power down and prepare for "sanitization." * **Key Beat:** Vance tries to bargain. The Rep hangs up. A missile impact rocks the ship. * **Human Element:** The horror comes from the Rep's indifference. He isn't angry; he's just filing a claim. **Chapter 5: The Ghost in the Machine** * **Focus:** The Turning Point. * **Plot:** The ship is dead in the water. Boarding claws latch onto the hull. Vance runs to the cargo bay. He intends to offer the droid to the boarding party to save his crew. * **Key Beat:** The Droid (Owen) is listening to the hull vibrations. He recognizes the magnetic signature of the boarding claws. It triggers a memory file. He looks at Vance and calls him by a different name (his dead partner's). "They've cornered us again, Billy." * **Action:** Vance sees the delusion but exploits it. He hands the Droid a gun. "Yeah. They have. Let's clear the way." It’s a manipulative choice, not a heroic one. **Chapter 6: Iron Sights** * **Focus:** Quiet before the Storm. * **Plot:** A brief lull while the boarders cut through the airlock. Vance and Owen wait in the corridor behind an overturned table. * **Key Beat:** No philosophy. Owen complains about the quality of the gun Vance gave him; Vance complains about Owen’s rust getting on the deck. In this mundane bickering, a bond forms. Owen admits his gyros represent a "standing target" and asks Vance to aim for the gaps in his armor if he gets hacked. * **Subtext:** They both know they are likely dying in the next ten minutes. **Chapter 7: Breach** * **Focus:** Visceral Action. * **Plot:** The airlock blows. The Syndicate commandos enter—clean, faceless, high-tech. * **Action:** It’s a massacre. The *Copperhead* is torn apart by stray fire. Steam burns, deafening noise. Owen moves like a freight train, absorbing hits that would vaporize a human. He is brutal, efficient, and terrifying. * **The Sacrifice:** Owen takes a heavy anti-tank round meant for Mara. His chassis shatters. He doesn't have a dying monologue; he just creates a wall of wreckage with his own body so Vance can get the kill shot on the leader. **Chapter 8: Dry Rations** * **Focus:** The Cost. * **Plot:** The aftermath. Drifting in space, waiting for life support to recharge. The Syndicate ship has retreated, deeming the asset destroyed. * **The Resolution:** Owen is a mess of wires on the floor. The smart financial move is to strip him for parts to fix the ship's engine. Vance stands over him with a wrench. * **The Twist:** Cut to two weeks later. The ship is flying, but barely. The crew is eating algae paste because they have no money. In the corner of the bridge, Owen sits, welded back together with mismatched scrap metal. He can no longer walk or shoot—he's just a static torso hooked into the nav-computer, grumbling about their flight path. * **Final Image:** Vance looks at the "worthless" pile of junk that saved his life, then out at the stars. They are broke, hungry, and hunted, but the family is intact.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
Here are the character profiles, designed to be gritty, flawed, and distinct. # Captain Silas "Rusty" Vance **Background & Personality:** Vance isn't a fallen hero; he's a former logistics officer for the losing side of the civil war (The Separatists). He didn't fight on the front lines; he cooked books and diverted fuel. He carries the guilt of survival, not the glory of combat. He is functionally depressed but masks it with a veneer of abrasive cynicism. He is driven by debt—specifically a loan shark "lien" on his ship that eats 80% of his income. * **Flaw:** He is transactional to a fault. He views people and objects in terms of their scrap value or utility. He will hesitate to save a life if it costs him fuel, not out of malice, but out of a deeply ingrained scarcity mindset. * **Idiosyncrasies:** He chews on caffeine dermal patches instead of wearing them on his skin, leaving him with stained teeth and a jittery, chemical intensity. He habitually checks the time on a wrist-chrono that has been broken for three years. **Physicality:** Mid-40s, but looks 60. "Spacer’s Spine" (a stoop from too many high-g burns without proper acceleration couches). He has a permanent layer of grime in his fingernails that no amount of scrubbing removes. He wears a flight jacket that is more duct tape than leather. He doesn't walk; he stomps, utilizing the magnetic soles of his boots heavily because he keeps the ship’s artificial gravity set to 0.8G to save power. **Motives & Relationships:** * **Motive:** To pay off the *Copperhead* so he can eventually sell it and retire to a planet with an atmosphere that doesn't taste like recycled farts. * **Allegiances:** To the Ship first, the Crew second, himself third. Everyone else is a threat or a customer. * **Relationship to Crew:** He treats Jax like a younger brother he doesn't respect and Mara like a landlord he’s afraid of. **Voice Sample:** * *(Internal Monologue)*: "Fuel gauge says 4%. Life support says 12%. Coffee pot says go to hell. If I sell the kidneys out of the pilot, I might make rent. Just kidding. Kidneys are low market right now." * *(Dialogue)*: "Mara, I don't pay you to tell me it’s impossible. I pay you—well, I *owe* you—to make the engine ignore the laws of physics for ten more minutes. Kick it if you have to." # Unit 0-N3 ("Owen") **Background & Personality:** A heavy-assault law enforcement chassis from the pre-war era. He wasn't built for AI complexity, but decades of running without a memory wipe has caused "data-rot," granting him a ghost of a personality. He thinks he is still a Deputy Marshal in a frontier town, but his facial recognition software is glitched, often overlaying faces from his past onto current people. He is rigid, literal, and terrifyingly violent when "protocols" are breached. * **Flaw:** He is delusional. He doesn't realize he is on a spaceship half the time; he thinks he is in a dusty mining settlement. * **Idiosyncrasy:** His audio emitter is damaged. His voice sounds like it’s coming through a blown-out speaker, and he occasionally emits a high-pitched feedback whine when confused. He tries to tip a hat that he isn't wearing. **Physicality:** Seven feet of rusted, pitted durasteel. He is ugly—utilitarian, boxy, covered in "void-scale" (frozen localized condensation and mineral dust). His left gyroscope is misaligned, meaning he lists heavily to the left when walking, correcting with a loud pneumatic hiss. His "eyes" are just optical sensors behind a frag-mesh cage, glowing a dim, failing amber. **Motives & Relationships:** * **Motive:** To uphold "The Code" (a corrupted list of municipal bylaws and martial law decrees). * **Allegiances:** To the Law (abstract). * **Relationship to Crew:** He perceives the crew as "civilians in a combat zone" that require protection, even if he arrested them five minutes prior. **Voice Sample:** * *(Internal Monologue)*: [ERROR: MEMORY CORRUPTION. SECTOR 7G. TARGET ACQUIRED. PROTECT THE WITNESS.] * *(Dialogue)*: "You have the right to remain... *[static screech]* ...silent. Please keep your hands where I can... *[gear grinding noise]* ...see them. You remind me of Billy. Billy was a good man. Before the fire." # Jax ( The Pilot) **Background & Personality:** A "Void-born." Jax grew up on a zero-g orbital station and never touched dirt until they were twenty. As a result, they have incredible spatial awareness in space but are clumsy and agoraphobic on planetary surfaces. Jax acts as the ship’s nervous system—flighty, superstitious, and high-energy. They use stimulants to stay awake for 30-hour shifts. * **Flaw:** Cowardice. Jax’s first instinct is always to run. In a fight, Jax will hide. * **Idiosyncrasy:** Names every asteroid they dodge. Taps rhythms on the flight console to "soothe the machine spirit." Believes the ship has feelings. **Physicality:** Tall, unnaturally thin, and pale (elongated limbs from low-gravity development). Moving with a fluid, spider-like grace in zero-g but looking awkward and hunching under gravity. Large, dark eyes that blink too often. Wears a flight suit three sizes too big, covered in hand-drawn lucky symbols. **Motives & Relationships:** * **Motive:** Survival. Jax stays on the *Copperhead* because Vance is the only captain who tolerates their neuroses. * **Allegiances:** To Vance (codependent). * **Relationship to Crew:** Jax fears Mara and seeks validation from Vance. **Voice Sample:** * *(Dialogue)*: "She’s bucking, Cap! The port thruster hates me today. I told you we shouldn't have flown through that nebula on a Tuesday. Tuesday nebulas are bad juju. We’re gonna die, we’re gonna die, oh look, a comet." # Mara (The Mechanic) **Background & Personality:** A disgraced union dockworker. She knows machines better than people. Silence is her default state. She is the physical anchor of the crew—where Jax is flighty and Vance is scheming, Mara is immovable. She holds the ship together with spit, prayer, and illegal welds. She has a criminal record for assaulting a foreman who cut safety protocols. * **Flaw:** Stubbornness. Once she decides something, she cannot be moved. She holds grudges for years. She secretly despises Vance’s leadership but loves the ship too much to leave. * **Idiosyncrasy:** She names her tools but not the ship. She drinks industrial coolant alcohol (distilled radiator fluid) because regular booze "tastes like water." **Physicality:** Short, stocky, built like a brick wall. Her skin is a map of burn scars and grease tattoos. She wears a welding mask pushed up on her forehead like a crown at all times. Her hands are calloused to the point of feeling like leather gloves. She carries a massive Kinetic Hammer—a tool for percussive maintenance that doubles as a weapon. **Motives & Relationships:** * **Motive:** To keep the *Copperhead* flying. She sees the ship as a living beast that is being mistreated by Vance. * **Allegiances:** To the *Copperhead*. * **Relationship to Crew:** She acts as the enforcer. When Vance needs someone intimidated, he stands behind Mara. **Voice Sample:** * *(Internal Monologue)*: "Idiot thinks he can route power from life support to shields without blowing the manifold. He’s going to cook us alive. I should let him." * *(Dialogue)*: "It’s not fixed. It’s bypassed. Don't touch the red switch. If you touch the red switch, the engine vents into the bridge and we all boil. Understand? Good."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: The Dead Weight The *Copperhead* groaned, a low, metallic complaint that vibrated through the deck plating and straight into Captain Silas "Rusty" Vance’s molars. He shifted in his command chair, trying to find a posture that didn't aggrevate the pinched nerve in his lower back, and failed. "She’s running hot, Cap," Jax said from the helm. The pilot was curled into a ball in the oversized chair, knees pulled to chest, pale fingers dancing over the navigation console like spiders on a web. "Thermal couplings on port-side are screaming. I can hear them." "You can't hear a coupling, Jax," Vance muttered, rubbing his eyes. The bridge smelled of stale ozone and the bitter, chemical tang of the caffeine patch he was currently chewing on. "It’s a vacuum outside." "I hear them in my *soul*," Jax whispered, tapping a rhythmic beat on the dash. "Thump-thump-hiss. Bad juju. We shouldn't have taken the Boneyard route." Vance looked out the viewport. The Boneyard was a graveyard of the Expansion Era—a dense field of shattered asteroids and the drifting husks of mining trawlers that had failed to make quota. It was dark, dangerous, and effectively invisible to long-range scanners. Which was exactly why they were here. "We took the Boneyard because we can’t afford the toll on the hyper-lane," Vance reminded him. He checked the fuel gauge. Four percent. A flashing red light on the console blinked in time with his throbbing headache. "Besides, the Client paid for discretion. Discretion means dodging the patrols." "The Client paid for a suicide run," a voice crackled over the comms. Hard, flat, and distorted by the interference of the asteroid field. Vance hit the talk button. "Mara, I pay you to fix the ship, not critique my business model. How’s the cargo?" "Secure," the mechanic replied. "But the mag-locks are drawing too much power. That crate is heavy, Vance. Denser than lead. It’s messing with the gravity plating." "Just keep it locked down. We’re three hours from the drop." Vance leaned back, the leather of his jacket creaking. He looked at the manifest on his screen for the hundredth time. *Item: Class-5 Containment Unit. Origin: Mining Outpost Delta-9. Contents: CLASSIFIED. Handling Instructions: Do Not Open.* It was the "Do Not Open" part that usually signaled a payday. In the fringe systems, closed boxes meant illegal tech, stolen art, or bio-hazards. Vance didn't care which. The advance payment had covered the fuel to get here, and the completion bonus would cover the interest on the lien the Syndicate held on his engines. If he didn't make this drop, the *Copperhead* would be repossessed, and likely stripped for parts with him still inside it. "Contact!" Jax yelped, sitting bolt upright. "Passive sensors just pinged. Twelve thousand clicks off the starboard bow. Something big." Vance sat up, spitting the chewed-up caffeine patch onto the floor. "Patrol?" "No transponder," Jax said, eyes wide, reflecting the green glow of the scope. "It’s drifting. No heat signature. Just mass. Wait... it’s turning." "Debris doesn't turn, Jax." "I know debris doesn't turn! That's why I'm yelling!" Vance punched the intercom. "Mara! Kill the running lights. Divert everything to the stealth drive. We’ve got a bogey." The ship plunged into darkness as the interior lights died, replaced by the dim, blood-red glow of the emergency floods. The hum of the recyclers cut out. The silence that filled the ship was heavy, suffocating. "Drifting," Vance whispered. "Match its tumble." Jax’s hands were a blur. The *Copperhead* rolled, its thrusters firing in microscopic bursts of compressed gas. Through the viewport, a shadow blotted out the stars. It was a scavenger wreck—a jagged hulk of metal, bristling with magnetic grappling claws. A pirate skiff, likely lying in wait for idiots trying to shortcut through the Boneyard. "They haven't seen us," Vance murmured. "We’re just a rock to them. An ugly, metal rock." They drifted past the pirate vessel, close enough that Vance could see the blast scars on its hull. Minutes stretched like hours. The only sound on the bridge was Jax’s terrified, shallow breathing. When the pirate ship was finally a speck in the rear optical sensors, Vance let out a breath he felt like he’d been holding since Tuesday. "Power up," he ordered. "Slowly. Don't spike the reactor." The lights flickered back to life. The hum of the ship returned. "I hate this job," Jax whimpered, uncurling slightly. "I want to go back to orbital shuttling. Nice, safe tourists. No pirates." "Tourists don't pay double for rush delivery," Vance said, though his hands were shaking slightly as he reached for a fresh caffeine patch. "Mara, sound off. Did the gravity cut shift the load?" There was a long pause. Static hissed over the line. "Mara?" "I'm here," the mechanic’s voice came back, lower this time. Tenser. "Captain. You need to come down here." Vance frowned. "I'm busy flying a ship on fumes, Mara. If a strap broke, fix it." "It’s not a strap." The tone in her voice made the hairs on Vance’s arms stand up. Mara didn't get scared. She got angry, she got violent, but she didn't get scared. "What is it?" "I was checking the seals on the crate," Mara said. "Put my hand on the side to check for temperature flux. Vance... it’s warm." "The cargo manifest said inert materials." "It’s warm," she repeated. "And... the mag-locks are rattling." Vance unbuckled his crash webbing. "Rattling? From the turbulence?" "No," Mara said. "From the inside." Vance froze. He looked at Jax, who had stopped tapping the console and was staring at the comms unit with wide, saucer-plate eyes. "Captain," Mara’s voice was barely a whisper now, cutting through the static like a razor. "Something’s... moving in that crate we picked up." Vance grabbed the kinetic shotgun clamped under the navigation console. He racked the slide. The sound was loud and ugly in the small space. "Jax, keep us steady," Vance said, his voice dropping into the flat, emotionless register of a man who had run out of good options years ago. "If the hull breaches, seal the bridge. If I don't come back in ten minutes, vent the hold." "Vent the hold?" Jax squeaked. "With you in it?" "Ideally, wait until I'm *not* in it," Vance said, heading for the hatch. "But I'm not picky." He stomped down the corridor, the magnetic soles of his boots changing the rhythm of his walk—*CLANG, step, CLANG, step.* The ship felt tighter than usual, the walls pressing in. The air grew colder as he descended toward the cargo bay. He reached the heavy blast door at the end of the spinal corridor. The panel was glowing amber—*Caution*. He punched in his override code. The hydraulics hissed, spitting a mist of hydraulic fluid, and the door groaned upward. The cargo bay was a cavern of shadows and rusted catwalks. In the center, bathed in the harsh glare of a single halogen work-light, sat the crate. It was a black, coffin-shaped monolith, stamped with warning sigils that had been deliberately scratched off. Mara stood twenty feet back, her massive kinetic hammer held across her chest like a shield. She didn't look at Vance. She stared at the box. Vance moved up beside her, shotgun raised. "Report." "It stopped for a second," Mara said, nodding at the crate. "Then it started again. Listen." Vance stepped closer. He held his breath. At first, there was nothing but the hum of the engine. Then, he heard it. A sound that didn't belong in a crate of mining equipment. *Thud.* It was heavy. Deliberate. *Thud.* The metal of the crate’s side bowed outward slightly, then popped back with a groan. Vance lowered the shotgun slightly, his mind racing through the calculations. A beast? A slave? A bomb with a timer? Whatever it was, it was awake. And if it was strong enough to dent plasteel alloy, it was going to get out. "We can't fly with a variable like this," Vance said, mostly to himself. "If it breaks loose during re-entry, it crashes the ship." "So we vent it?" Mara asked, tightening her grip on the hammer. Vance looked at the crate. He thought about the debt. He thought about the fact that the Syndicate paid for delivery, not debris. "If we vent it, we don't get paid," Vance said. "If we don't get paid, they take the ship." "If that thing eats us, we don't need the money," Mara countered. "Valid point." Vance spit on the deck. He stepped up to the control panel on the side of the crate. His finger hovered over the seal release. "Get ready," he said. "Creating an opening," Mara grunted, hefting the hammer. Vance punched the release code: *0-0-0-0*. The magnetic seals hissed—a sound like a dying snake. Pneumatic bolts fired with a series of loud cracks. The front panel of the crate didn't just open; it was kicked off. The heavy slab of metal flew across the bay and slammed into the far wall. From the darkness of the container, two glowing amber optics flared to life in the dust cloud. A metallic, grinding voice, distorted by years of decay, boomed out, filling the hold. "DROP... YOUR... WEAPONS."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me work through the key elements systematically. **Atmosphere and Voice** The chapter establishes its blue-collar, gritty space western atmosphere effectively. The sensory details are well-chosen and grounded: "stale ozone and the bitter, chemical tang of the caffeine patch he was currently chewing on," the "blood-red glow of the emergency floods," the hydraulics "spitting a mist of hydraulic fluid." These feel tactile and earned rather than decorative. The ship feels genuinely decrepit and lived-in. **Character Work** Vance is rendered well. His transactional thinking is shown through action and internal logic rather than stated: he calculates the debt implications before deciding whether to vent the hold. The detail of chewing caffeine patches rather than wearing them is deployed naturally. His final line about not being picky about whether he's in the hold when it's vented is perfectly in character—dark humor masking genuine fatalism.
